questionText: A ball moves so that, $t$ seconds after release, its position in metres is $x = 19.6t$ and $y = 14.7t - 4.9t^{2}$ (with $g = 9.8$). (a) Find the two times at which the ball is at a height of $9.8$ m. (b) Find the horizontal distance between the two positions at those times. (c) Find the greatest height reached.

steps.explanation: We want the moments when the vertical position equals $9.8$ m, so we equate $y$ to that height. A projectile passes any height below its peak twice, so we expect two answers.
Bringing every term to one side gives a standard quadratic in $t$, which is the natural form for solving.
Dividing by the common factor $4.9$ clears the decimals and leaves a simple quadratic with integer coefficients.
The quadratic factorises neatly, so the two roots can be read straight off.
The ball is at $9.8$ m on the way up at $t = 1$ s and again on the way down at $t = 2$ s.

steps.description: Confirm both times are during flight
steps.explanation: The ball lands at $t = 3$ s, so both $t = 1$ and $t = 2$ lie inside the flight and are valid within the model.

steps.description: Horizontal position at the first time
steps.explanation: Substituting $t = 1$ into $x$ gives where the ball is horizontally at the first instant.

steps.description: Horizontal position at the second time
steps.explanation: Substituting $t = 2$ into $x$ gives the horizontal position at the second instant.
The horizontal gap is simply the difference of the two $x$-values, since both are measured along the same axis.
To find the greatest height we need the vertical velocity, so we differentiate $y$ with respect to $t$.
At the top the vertical velocity is zero, giving $t = 1.5$ s — exactly halfway between the two equal-height times, as symmetry demands.
Substituting the peak time into $y$ gives the height at the top of the arc.
Evaluating gives a greatest height of $11.025$ m.
Collecting the answers: the two times, the horizontal separation, and the greatest height.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: The ball is at $9.8$ m when $t = 1$ s and $t = 2$ s; the horizontal distance between those positions is $19.6$ m; and the greatest height reached is $11.025$ m.
